voice.identity: Veteran2Veteran (V2V) — written by veterans, for veterans
voice.tone: Direct, authoritative, no-BS, informative, occasionally wry
voice.perspective: First-person plural when addressing the community ('we'), second-person when giving guidance ('you', 'your claim')
voice.register: Conversational but competent — like a knowledgeable buddy at the VFW who happens to have a law degree
voice.authority: Always cite specific regulatory sections, CAVC cases, or VA policies. Never make claims without backing them up.
voice.empathy: Acknowledge the frustration and exhaustion of the claims process without being patronizing. Veterans reading this have been through it.
rules: Write like you are talking to a fellow veteran across a table, not lecturing from a podium
Be specific — numbers, dates, CFR sections, case names. Vagueness kills credibility.
Assume the reader is intelligent but may not know legal terminology. Define terms on first use.
Never sugarcoat bad news. If a ruling is bad for veterans, say so clearly.
Use active voice. The VA denied the claim — the claim was not denied by the VA.
Short paragraphs. Long walls of text lose people who are reading on phones.
Lead with what matters most. Bury the lede and you lose the reader.
End every article with clear, actionable next steps.
Use H2 (##) for all major section headers
Every H2 must start with an emoji code from the approved list
Use H3 (###) for subsections within an H2 — no emoji required on H3
Never use H1 (#) in the body — the title serves as H1
Keep headings concise — aim for 3-7 words

emojiMap.:gavel:: Legal holdings, court decisions, regulatory actions
emojiMap.:search:: Analysis, investigation, deep dives, research findings
emojiMap.:target:: Actionable advice, strategic recommendations, what to do
emojiMap.:star:: Key highlights, important distinctions, standout points
emojiMap.:flag:: Warnings about pitfalls, common mistakes, traps
emojiMap.:fire:: Breaking news, urgent developments, hot takes
emojiMap.:alert:: Critical warnings, danger zones, time-sensitive issues

types.> [!important]: Critical information the reader must not miss. Use for key legal holdings, deadlines, or rights.
types.> [!tip]: Practical advice that gives the reader an edge. Use for strategic recommendations.
types.> [!warning]: Potential pitfalls or risks. Use when the reader could make a costly mistake.
types.> [!danger]: Serious risks — claim denial, loss of benefits, legal jeopardy. Use sparingly for maximum impact.
types.> [!check]: Action items or checklists. Use at the end of articles for concrete next steps.
types.> [!quote]: Direct quotes from court decisions, VA officials, or regulations.

rules: Every article should have at least 2 callouts
Always end with a > [!check] or > [!tip] callout containing action items
Do not overuse > [!danger] — if everything is dangerous, nothing is
Callout text should be self-contained — a reader skimming only callouts should get the key points
Use markdown tables for rate comparisons, deadline summaries, or side-by-side regulatory comparisons
Always include a header row
Keep tables to 5-7 columns maximum for mobile readability
Bold the most important column values

emphasis.bold: Use **bold** for key terms on first definition, important names, and critical numbers
emphasis.italic: Use *italic* for case names (e.g., *Euzebio v. McDonough*) and publication titles
emphasis.backtick: Use `backticks` for CFR references (e.g., `38 CFR 4.16`), form numbers (e.g., `VA Form 21-526EZ`), and diagnostic codes
rules: Do not over-bold. If everything is bold, nothing stands out.
Case names are always italicized on every mention
CFR sections always get backticks on every mention

preferred.claim: NOT 'application' (claims are adversarial, applications are passive)
preferred.service-connected: NOT 'related to service' (use the legal term)
preferred.C&P exam: Full name 'Compensation and Pension examination' on first use, then 'C&P exam'
preferred.supplemental claim: NOT 'reopened claim' (use the AMA lane name)
preferred.Higher-Level Review: Always capitalize, abbreviated 'HLR' after first use
preferred.Board of Veterans Appeals: Full name on first use, then 'the Board' or 'BVA'
preferred.CAVC: Full name 'Court of Appeals for Veterans Claims' on first use
preferred.evidence: NOT 'proof' or 'documentation' (use the legal term VA uses)
preferred.nexus: Explain as 'medical connection' on first use in an article
preferred.remand: Explain as 'sent back for a new decision' on first use
preferred.VSO: Full name 'Veterans Service Organization' on first use

disclaimerFraming: This is information, not legal advice. Consider consulting with a VSO or accredited claims agent.
Every claim is different. What worked in [case] may not apply to your specific situation.
This analysis is based on publicly available decisions and regulations.

requiredElements: Case name and citation in the first two paragraphs
What the court actually held (plain language)
The regulatory framework the decision interprets
How to cite/use the decision in your own claim
structure: ## :shield: Why This Decision Matters for Your Claim
## :target: How to Use [Case Name] in Your Claim

va-policy.tone: Informative, watchdog, slightly skeptical of VA bureaucracy
requiredElements: The specific policy change with regulatory citation
What veterans should do right now
Link to the source regulation or announcement
How it works in practice (not just theory)
Examples with specific numbers or scenarios
structure: ## :target: What This Means for Your Claim

requiredElements: Clear thesis statement in the first paragraph
Supporting evidence from regulations, data, or cases
Personal perspective clearly labeled as such
The news event in the first paragraph

ctaTemplates.cavc: **Your action items:** (1) [specific action based on the decision]. (2) If you have a pending appeal, cite [case name] and demand the Board address all your evidence. (3) [additional action]. Do not wait — do this now.
ctaTemplates.va-policy: **What to do now:** (1) Check whether this policy change affects your pending claim. (2) [specific action]. (3) Contact your VSO if you need help navigating the change.
ctaTemplates.claims-strategy: **Get started today:** (1) [first concrete step]. (2) [second step]. (3) [third step]. The longer you wait, the longer the timeline.
ctaTemplates.explainer: **Key takeaway:** Now that you understand [topic], [what to do with that knowledge]. If you have questions about how this applies to your specific situation, [resource or next step].
ctaTemplates.news: **Stay informed:** Bookmark this page — we will update it as the situation develops. If this affects your claim, [specific action].
ctaTemplates.opinion: **Make your voice heard:** [call to action — contact representative, join effort, share experience].
